data.content:
Homer
Homer is the ancient Greek poet credited with composing the Odyssey, which is referenced in several works.<SEP>Homer is the attributed author of the Odyssey, which recounts the story of Calypso detaining Odysseus on Ogygia.

Odyssey
The Odyssey is an epic poem attributed to Homer that describes, among other events, Calypso detaining Odysseus on Ogygia and his eventual release.

Dolóessa
Dolóessa is a Homeric epithet meaning 'subtle' or 'wily' that is associated with Calypso and used to justify her reclusive character.

Odysseus
Odysseus is a figure from ancient Greek mythology whose relationship with Calypso inspires accounts of her love and yearning.<SEP>Odysseus is a fabled Greek hero who is detained by Calypso on Ogygia for seven years, longs to return to his wife Penelope, and is eventually ordered freed by the gods.

Atlas
Atlas is a Titan who is generally said to be the father of Calypso.

Ogygia
Ogygia is the island where Calypso lives and where Odysseus is detained for seven years in the Odyssey.

Etymologicum Magnum
Etymologicum Magnum is a medieval dictionary that interprets Calypso's name as meaning 'concealing the knowledge'.

Greek Mythology
Greek Mythology is the body of myths from ancient Greece in which Calypso, Odysseus, and other gods and nymphs appear.

Immortality
Immortality is the state of living forever, which Calypso promises to grant Odysseus if he stays with her.

Fabulae
Fabulae is a work that describes Calypso as the daughter of Pleione, differing from other accounts of her parentage.

Pleione
Pleione is the mother of the Pleiades and, according to the Fabulae, the mother of Calypso.

Calypso
Calypso is the title of an entry in the 11th edition of Encyclopædia Britannica.<SEP>Calypso is a figure from ancient Greek mythology who is associated with love for Odysseus and is interpreted in literature and philosophy.<SEP>Calypso is a nymph in Greek mythology who lives on the island of Ogygia, detains Odysseus for seven years, offers him immortality, and is variously described as the daughter of Atlas, an Oceanid, a Nereid, or a daughter of Helios and Perse; she is associated with concealment and reclusiveness.

Hesiod
Hesiod is an ancient Greek poet whose works Theogony and The Shield and other fragments are referenced.<SEP>Hesiod is an ancient Greek poet who mentions a Calypso as one of the Oceanid nymphs, daughter of Tethys and Oceanus.

Homeric Hymn to Demeter
The Homeric Hymn to Demeter is an ancient Greek hymn whose anonymous author mentions a Calypso as one of the Oceanid nymphs.

Pleiades
Pleiades are the daughters of Pleione, mentioned as part of Pleione's family when describing Calypso's possible parentage.

Oceanid Nymphs
Oceanid nymphs are daughters of Tethys and Oceanus, among whom some sources include a Calypso.

Tethys
Tethys is a goddess who, with Oceanus, is parent to the Oceanid nymphs, including a Calypso in some accounts.

Oceanus
Oceanus is a god who, with Tethys, is parent to the Oceanid nymphs, including a Calypso in some accounts.

Nereus
Nereus is a sea god who, with Doris, is parent to the Nereids, among whom Calypso is sometimes listed.

Apollodorus
Apollodorus is an author associated with the work The Library, cited in the references concerning Calypso.<SEP>Apollodorus is an author who includes Calypso in a list of Nereids, daughters of Nereus and Doris.

Nereids
Nereids are sea nymphs, daughters of Nereus and Doris, and a group in which Apollodorus lists Calypso.

Helios
Helios is the sun-god who, with Perse, is said by John Tzetzes to be the father of Calypso and Circe.

John Tzetzes
John Tzetzes is a scholar who makes Calypso a daughter of Helios and Perse, linking her family to Circe.

Doris
Doris is a figure who, with Nereus, is parent to the Nereids, including Calypso in some accounts.

Perse
Perse is an Oceanid nymph who, with Helios, is said by John Tzetzes to be the mother of Calypso and Circe.

Circe
Circe is a goddess, daughter of Helios and Perse, associated with Odysseus and sometimes confused with Calypso due to similar behavior and connections.

Cephalonians
Cephalonians are a people said in a fragment from the Catalogue of Women to have been borne by Calypso to Hermes.

Catalogue of Women
Catalogue of Women is an ancient work from which a fragment reports that Calypso bore the Cephalonians to Hermes.

Athena
Athena is the patron goddess of Odysseus who asks Zeus to order his release from Calypso's island.

Zeus
Zeus is the chief god who orders Hermes to tell Calypso to set Odysseus free, declaring that Odysseus is not destined to live with her forever.

Penelope
Penelope is the wife of Odysseus, from whom he is separated while detained by Calypso and to whom he wishes to return.

Latinus
Latinus is a son who in some accounts is borne by Calypso to Odysseus, though more commonly attributed to Circe.

Nausinous
Nausinous is one of two children that some accounts say Calypso bore to Odysseus.

Nausithous
Nausithous is one of two children that some accounts say Calypso bore to Odysseus.

Hermes
Hermes is a god who visits Calypso's island in the Odyssey, is ordered by Zeus to tell her to free Odysseus, and in one fragment fathers the Cephalonians with her.

Gilgamesh
Gilgamesh is a hero whose interactions with Siduri in the Epic of Gilgamesh are compared to the story of Odysseus and Calypso.

Poseidon
Poseidon is the sea-god who hates Odysseus for blinding his son Polyphemus and in one fragment is said to have Calypso detain Odysseus as a favour.

Epic of Gilgamesh
The Epic of Gilgamesh is an ancient Mesopotamian epic in which Gilgamesh meets Siduri, a scene compared to Odysseus and Calypso.

Siduri
Siduri is a female figure in the Epic of Gilgamesh who interacts with Gilgamesh in a way compared to Calypso's dealings with Odysseus.

Hyginus
Hyginus is an author who reported that Calypso killed herself because of her love for Odysseus.<SEP>Hyginus is an author who reports that Calypso killed herself because of her love for Odysseus.

Polyphemus
Polyphemus is a cyclops, son of Poseidon, who is blinded by Odysseus and whose injury causes Poseidon to detest Odysseus.

Cyclops
Cyclops is the race of one-eyed giants to which Polyphemus belongs.

Calypso Watching the Ocean
Calypso Watching the Ocean is a poem by Letitia Landon that portrays Calypso as eternally yearning for Odysseus' return and comments on the folly of such obsession.

Ryan Patrick Hanley
Ryan Patrick Hanley is a philosopher who commented on the interpretation of Calypso in Fénelon's Les Aventures de Télémaque and linked her story to Eros and pride.<SEP>Ryan Patrick Hanley is a philosopher who commented on interpretations of Calypso in Les Aventures de Télé.

Les Aventures de Télé
Les Aventures de Télé is a work in which interpretations of Calypso are discussed, referenced by Ryan Patrick Hanley.

Les Aventures de Télémaque (implied)
Les Aventures de Télémaque is the likely full title implied by Les Aventures de Télé, associated with philosophical interpretations of Calypso.

Letitia Landon
Letitia Landon is a poet who wrote the poem "Calypso Watching the Ocean" describing Calypso's yearning for Odysseus.<SEP>Letitia Landon is a poet who wrote Calypso Watching the Ocean, depicting Calypso's endless yearning for Odysseus.

The Odyssey (Calypso Episode)
The Odyssey (Calypso Episode) is the portion of the Odyssey that narrates Calypso's detention of Odysseus, his longing for home, and his eventual release by divine order.

She Who Conceals
She Who Conceals is the literal meaning of the name Calypso, referring to her role in hiding Odysseus from the world.

Concealing the Knowledge
Concealing the Knowledge is an interpretation of Calypso's name given by the Etymologicum Magnum, highlighting her association with hidden understanding.

Kalyptō
Kalyptō is the Ancient Greek verb καλύπτω meaning 'to cover', 'to conceal', or 'to hide', from which the name Calypso is derived.

Wine
Wine is one of the provisions Calypso supplies to Odysseus for his journey when he leaves Ogygia.

Tools For Boatbuilding
Tools For Boatbuilding collectively refers to the axe, drill, and adze that Calypso gives Odysseus to build his boat.

Bread
Bread is one of the food items Calypso provides to Odysseus as he prepares to depart Ogygia.

Dolóessa (Epithet)
Dolóessa (epithet) is the Homeric descriptive term meaning 'subtle' or 'wily' applied to Calypso, emphasizing her deceptive and reclusive traits.

Clothing
Clothing is part of the supplies Calypso gives Odysseus for his voyage after releasing him.

Ancient Greece
Ancient Greece is the cultural and historical context in which the meaning of Calypso has been philosophically discussed.

Honoured People
Honoured People refers to the special class of people beyond the sea to whom the hero-wanderer is sent in the comparison between the stories of Odysseus with Calypso and Gilgamesh with Siduri.

Poem "Calypso Watching The Ocean"
The poem "Calypso Watching the Ocean" is a literary work that portrays Calypso as eternally yearning for Odysseus' return and critiques the folly of such obsession.

Philosophers
Philosophers are thinkers who have written about the meaning of Calypso in the world of ancient Greece.

Fénelon
Fénelon is the author of Les Aventures de Télémaque, which contains a depiction of Calypso analyzed by philosophers.

Other Materials For Boat
Other Materials For Boat refers to the unspecified additional materials that Calypso provides to Odysseus to complete his boat.

Les Aventures De Télémaque
Les Aventures de Télémaque is a literary work written by Fénelon that includes an interpretation of Calypso discussed by philosophers.

Eros
Eros is a concept of love or desire that Ryan Patrick Hanley associates with the story of Calypso as linked to pride.

Pride
Pride is a concept that Ryan Patrick Hanley connects with Eros in interpreting the story of Calypso.

"Bourgeois Housewives" Sensibility
The "bourgeois housewives" sensibility is a conceptual characterization that Adorno and Horkheimer associate with the depiction of Calypso.

Max Horkheimer
Max Horkheimer is a philosopher who, with Theodor Adorno, analyzed the depiction of Calypso as combining power over fate with bourgeois housewife sensibility.

Power Over Fate
Power over fate is a conceptual element in the depiction of Calypso highlighted by Adorno and Horkheimer.

Theodor Adorno
Theodor Adorno is a philosopher who, with Max Horkheimer, discussed Calypso in terms of power over fate and bourgeois housewife sensibility.

Calypso (Mythology)
Calypso (mythology) is the encyclopedic article or entry that describes the nymph Calypso, her etymology, family, mythology, and later interpretations.

The Library
The Library is a work by Apollodorus referenced in relation to Greek mythology including Calypso.

James George Frazer
James George Frazer is the translator of Apollodorus' The Library in the referenced English edition.

Stephanie L. Budin
Stephanie L. Budin is the author of Intimate Lives of the Ancient Greeks, cited in the references.

Perseus Digital Library
The Perseus Digital Library is an online resource that hosts digital versions of classical texts such as The Library and other works cited.

Thomas Bulfinch
Thomas Bulfinch is the author of The Age of Fable: Stories of Gods and Heroes, referenced in the list.

data.entity_name: Intimate Lives Of The Ancient Greeks
data.content:
Intimate Lives Of The Ancient Greeks
Intimate Lives of the Ancient Greeks is a book by Stephanie L. Budin referenced in connection with ancient Greek topics.

data.entity_name: The Age Of Fable: Stories Of Gods And Heroes
data.content:
The Age Of Fable: Stories Of Gods And Heroes
The Age of Fable: Stories of Gods and Heroes is a book by Thomas Bulfinch that retells mythological stories and is cited in the references.

Floating Press
Floating Press is the publisher of a referenced edition of The Age of Fable: Stories of Gods and Heroes.

Richard Caldwell
Richard Caldwell is associated with a referenced edition or study of Hesiod's Theogony.

Hesiod's Theogony (Caldwell Edition)
Hesiod's Theogony in the edition by Richard Caldwell is a referenced work related to Greek mythology.

data.entity_name: Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia Of The Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2, Ark – Cas
data.content:
Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia Of The Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2, Ark – Cas
Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia of the Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2, Ark – Cas is a reference volume containing the article on Calypso.

Focus Publishing/R. Pullins Company
Focus Publishing/R. Pullins Company is the publisher of Richard Caldwell's edition of Hesiod's Theogony.

Paul Dräger
Paul Dräger is the author of the article "Calypso" in Brill's New Pauly cited in the references.

"Calypso" (Brill's New Pauly Article)
The article "Calypso" in Brill's New Pauly is a scholarly encyclopedia entry on Calypso written by Paul Dräger.

Hubert Cancik
Hubert Cancik is an editor of Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia of the Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2.

Brill
Brill is the publishing company that publishes Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia of the Ancient World.

Helmuth Schneider
Helmuth Schneider is an editor of Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia of the Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2.

Renaud Gagné
Renaud Gagné is the author of Cosmography and the Idea of Hyperborea in Ancient Greece: A Philology of Worlds, cited in the references.

data.entity_name: Cosmography And The Idea Of Hyperborea In Ancient Greece: A Philology Of Worlds
data.content:
Cosmography And The Idea Of Hyperborea In Ancient Greece: A Philology Of Worlds
Cosmography and the Idea of Hyperborea in Ancient Greece: A Philology of Worlds is a scholarly book by Renaud Gagné referenced in the list.

Cambridge University Press
Cambridge University Press is a publishing organization associated with the Encyclopædia Britannica (11th Edition).<SEP>Cambridge University Press is the publisher of several referenced works, including texts by Renaud Gagné and others.

Pierre Grimal
Pierre Grimal is the author of The Dictionary of Classical Mythology, which includes an entry on Calypso and is cited in the references.

data.entity_name: The Raft Of Odysseus: The Ethnographic Imagination Of Homer's Odyssey
data.content:
The Raft Of Odysseus: The Ethnographic Imagination Of Homer's Odyssey
The Raft of Odysseus: The Ethnographic Imagination of Homer's Odyssey is a scholarly work by Carol Dougherty discussing the Odyssey.

Wiley-Blackwell
Wiley-Blackwell is the publisher of Pierre Grimal's The Dictionary of Classical Mythology.

Carol Dougherty
Carol Dougherty is the author of The Raft of Odysseus: The Ethnographic Imagination of Homer's Odyssey, cited in the references.

The Dictionary Of Classical Mythology
The Dictionary of Classical Mythology is a reference work by Pierre Grimal that contains an entry on Calypso.

Oxford University Press
Oxford University Press is a publishing organization that published Hesiod: Theogony.<SEP>Oxford University Press is a publisher of classical studies such as The Raft of Odysseus and works on Hesiod.

I.B. Tauris
I.B. Tauris is the publisher of Edith Hall's The Return of Ulysses: A Cultural History of Homer's Odyssey.

Edith Hall
Edith Hall is the author of The Return of Ulysses: A Cultural History of Homer's Odyssey, cited in the references.

data.entity_name: The Routledge Handbook Of Greek Mythology
data.content:
The Routledge Handbook Of Greek Mythology
The Routledge Handbook of Greek Mythology is a reference work by Robin Hard based on H.J. Rose's Handbook of Greek Mythology.

data.entity_name: The Return Of Ulysses: A Cultural History Of Homer's Odyssey
data.content:
The Return Of Ulysses: A Cultural History Of Homer's Odyssey
The Return of Ulysses: A Cultural History of Homer's Odyssey is a book by Edith Hall exploring the cultural impact of the Odyssey.

Robin Hard
Robin Hard is the author of The Routledge Handbook of Greek Mythology, based on H.J. Rose's Handbook.

Psychology Press
Psychology Press is the publisher of The Routledge Handbook of Greek Mythology.

Theogony (Hesiod)
Theogony is a work by Hesiod, referenced in editions and translations cited in the text.

data.entity_name: The Shield. Catalogue Of Women. Other Fragments (Hesiod)
data.content:
The Shield. Catalogue Of Women. Other Fragments (Hesiod)
The Shield. Catalogue of Women. Other Fragments is a collection of Hesiodic works edited and translated by Glenn W. Most.

Hugh G. Evelyn-White
Hugh G. Evelyn-White is the translator and editor of The Homeric Hymns and Homerica and Hesiod's Theogony in a referenced edition.

data.entity_name: The Homeric Hymns And Homerica (Hesiod Edition)
data.content:
The Homeric Hymns And Homerica (Hesiod Edition)
The Homeric Hymns and Homerica with an English Translation by Hugh G. Evelyn-White is a collection including Hesiodic and related works referenced in the list.

Glenn W. Most
Glenn W. Most is the editor and translator of The Shield. Catalogue of Women. Other Fragments in the Loeb Classical Library.

Loeb Classical Library
Loeb Classical Library is a series that publishes classical texts with translation, including Hesiod's works edited by Glenn W. Most.

A.T. Murray
A.T. Murray is the translator of Homer's The Odyssey in a referenced two-volume edition.

Hyginus' Fabulae
Hyginus' Fabulae is a handbook of Greek mythology by Hyginus, included in the volume Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae.

R. Scott Smith
R. Scott Smith is a translator and introducer of Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae.

Homeric Hymn To Demeter (2)
Homeric Hymn to Demeter (2) is a hymn included in The Homeric Hymns and Homerica translated by Hugh G. Evelyn-White.

Stephen M. Trzaskoma
Stephen M. Trzaskoma is a translator and introducer of Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae.

Merkelbach
Merkelbach is a co-editor of Fragmenta Hesiodea, a collection of Hesiod fragments.

Hackett Publishing Company
Hackett Publishing Company is the publisher of Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae: Two Handbooks of Greek Mythology.

Fragmenta Hesiodea
Fragmenta Hesiodea is a collection of fragments of Hesiod's works edited by Merkelbach and M. L. West.

M. L. West
M. L. West is a co-editor of Fragmenta Hesiodea and author of a separate work on Hesiod's Theogony.

Isaak Tzetzes
Isaak Tzetzes is a commentator whose marginal notes appear in the scholia to Lycophron's Alexandra.

Clarendon Press Oxford
Clarendon Press Oxford is the publisher of Fragmenta Hesiodea.

Scholia To Lycophron's Alexandra
Scholia to Lycophron's Alexandra are marginal notes by Isaak and Ioannis Tzetzes and others on Lycophron's work, referenced in the list.

Eduard Scheer
Eduard Scheer is the editor of a Greek edition of Lycophron's Alexandra that includes the scholia by Isaak and Ioannis Tzetzes.

Ioannis Tzetzes
Ioannis Tzetzes is a commentator whose marginal notes appear in the scholia to Lycophron's Alexandra.

The Odyssey
The Odyssey is an epic poem by Homer, referenced in the text and central to discussions of Odysseus and Calypso.

Topos Text Project
Topos Text Project is an online platform that hosts the scholia to Lycophron's Alexandra.

Weidmann
Weidmann is the publisher of Eduard Scheer's Greek edition of Lycophron's Alexandra.

data.entity_name: Dictionary Of Greek And Roman Biography And Mythology
data.content:
Dictionary Of Greek And Roman Biography And Mythology
Dictionary of Greek and Roman Biography and Mythology is a reference work by William Smith containing an entry on Calypso.

Archive.org
Archive.org is a digital library that provides access to Greek texts such as those related to Lycophron's Alexandra.

Thomas Van Nortwick
Thomas Van Nortwick is the author of The Unknown Odysseus: Alternate Worlds in Homers Odyssey.

data.entity_name: The Unknown Odysseus: Alternate Worlds In Homers Odyssey
data.content:
The Unknown Odysseus: Alternate Worlds In Homers Odyssey
The Unknown Odysseus: Alternate Worlds in Homers Odyssey is a book by Thomas Van Nortwick exploring alternative perspectives on the Odyssey.

William Smith
William Smith is the author of the Dictionary of Greek and Roman Biography and Mythology, which includes an entry on Calypso.

University Of Michigan Press
University of Michigan Press is the publisher of The Unknown Odysseus: Alternate Worlds in Homers Odyssey.

Hugh Chisholm
Hugh Chisholm is the editor of the 11th edition of Encyclopædia Britannica, which includes an entry on Calypso.

Encyclopædia Britannica (11th Edition)
Encyclopædia Britannica (11th Edition) is a reference work that includes an entry titled Calypso edited in 1911.<SEP>Encyclopædia Britannica (11th ed.) is a general encyclopedia edited by Hugh Chisholm, containing an article on Calypso.

Love For Odysseus
Love for Odysseus is the obsessive affection attributed to Calypso, described as the reason for her suicide and her eternal yearning for his return.

Cambridge University Press (Encyclopædia Britannica Publisher)
Cambridge University Press is the publisher of the 11th edition of Encyclopædia Britannica mentioned in the external links.

Folly Of Obsession
Folly of obsession is a critical idea expressed in the poem "Calypso Watching the Ocean," commenting on the irrationality of Calypso's obsessive yearning for Odysseus.

Hesiod: Theogony (West 1966)
Hesiod: Theogony (1966) is a scholarly edition of Hesiod's Theogony by M. L. West cited in the references.

Meaning Of Calypso
Meaning of Calypso is a philosophical topic concerning what Calypso represents in the world of ancient Greece.

data.entity_name: Apollodorus' Library And Hyginus' Fabulae: Two Handbooks Of Greek Mythology
data.content:
Apollodorus' Library And Hyginus' Fabulae: Two Handbooks Of Greek Mythology
Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae: Two Handbooks of Greek Mythology is a volume containing translations and introductions of Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae by R. Scott Smith and Stephen M. Trzaskoma.

data.entity_name: Power Over Fate And Bourgeois Housewives Sensibility
data.content:
Power Over Fate And Bourgeois Housewives Sensibility
Power over fate and bourgeois housewives sensibility is a combined conceptual characterization used by Adorno and Horkheimer to describe aspects of Calypso's depiction.

London
London is a city listed as a publishing location for several referenced classical works.

Harvard University Press
Harvard University Press is a publishing house based in Cambridge, Massachusetts, that publishes multiple classical texts referenced in the list.

William Heinemann Ltd.
William Heinemann Ltd. is a London-based publishing company that co-publishes several referenced classical works with Harvard University Press.

Oxford [England]
Oxford [England] is the location associated with Oxford University Press in the publishing details of The Raft of Odysseus.

Cambridge, Massachusetts
Cambridge, Massachusetts is a city listed as a publishing location for several referenced classical works.

Ann Arbor
Ann Arbor is the location associated with the publication of a work by the University of Michigan Press.<SEP>Ann Arbor is the city associated with University of Michigan Press in the publishing details of The Unknown Odysseus.

Oxford University Press, Incorporated
Oxford University Press, Incorporated is the corporate form of Oxford University Press cited as publisher of The Raft of Odysseus.

Praeger Publications
Praeger publications is the publishing house of Stephanie L. Budin's Intimate Lives of the Ancient Greeks.

ISBN 978-0-313-38571-1 is an International Standard Book Number identifying Intimate Lives of the Ancient Greeks.

ISBN 978-1-77652-441-9
ISBN 978-1-77652-441-9 is an International Standard Book Number for a referenced edition of The Age of Fable: Stories of Gods and Heroes.

ISBN 978-0-941051-00-2
ISBN 978-0-941051-00-2 is an International Standard Book Number for Richard Caldwell's edition of Hesiod's Theogony.

ISBN 9004122656
ISBN 9004122656 is an International Standard Book Number for Brill's New Pauly: Encyclopaedia of the Ancient World. Antiquity, Volume 2.

OCLC 693781068
OCLC 693781068 is a library catalog identifier associated with Edith Hall's The Return of Ulysses in the references.

ISBN 978-0-631-20102-1
ISBN 978-0-631-20102-1 is an International Standard Book Number for Pierre Grimal's The Dictionary of Classical Mythology.

ISBN 978-1-4416-8132-4
ISBN 978-1-4416-8132-4 is an International Standard Book Number associated with Edith Hall's The Return of Ulysses.

ISBN 978-1-108-83323-3
ISBN 978-1-108-83323-3 is an International Standard Book Number for Renaud Gagné's Cosmography and the Idea of Hyperborea in Ancient Greece.

ISBN 978-0-674-99623-6
ISBN 978-0-674-99623-6 is an International Standard Book Number for Glenn W. Most's Loeb edition The Shield. Catalogue of Women. Other Fragments.

ISBN 0-19-814169-6
ISBN 0-19-814169-6 is an international standard book number identifying the publication Hesiod: Theogony.<SEP>ISBN 0-19-814169-6 is an International Standard Book Number for M. L. West's 1966 edition Hesiod: Theogony.

ISBN 978-0-472-02521-3
ISBN 978-0-472-02521-3 is an international standard book number identifying a publication by the University of Michigan Press.

ISBN 978-0-198-14171-6
ISBN 978-0-198-14171-6 is an International Standard Book Number for Fragmenta Hesiodea.

ISBN 978-0-87220-821-6
ISBN 978-0-87220-821-6 is an International Standard Book Number for Apollodorus' Library and Hyginus' Fabulae: Two Handbooks of Greek Mythology.

Homers Odyssey
Homers Odyssey is a literary work referenced in connection with alternate worlds.

Hesiod: Theogony
Hesiod: Theogony is a work authored by West, M. L. and published in 1966.

University of Michigan Press
University of Michigan Press is a publishing organization based in Ann Arbor.

West, M. L.
West, M. L. is an author associated with the 1966 publication Hesiod: Theogony.

data.entity_name: Us: Alternate Worlds In Homers Odyssey
data.content:
Us: Alternate Worlds In Homers Odyssey
Us: Alternate Worlds In Homers Odyssey is a scholarly work related to alternate worlds in Homers Odyssey, published by the University of Michigan Press.

Chisholm, Hugh
Chisholm, Hugh is an editor associated with the 1911 entry on Calypso in Encyclopædia Britannica.

11th Edition
11th Edition refers to the specific edition number of Encyclopædia Britannica that includes the Calypso entry.

1911
1911 is the publication year of the Calypso entry in the 11th edition of Encyclopædia Britannica.

Odysseus's Boat
Calypso provides Odysseus with tools, materials, and supplies to build and provision a boat for his departure.

Odysseus's Departure
After seven years and the intervention of the gods, Calypso allows Odysseus to build a boat and leave her island.
